title: Introduction
description: NOC 62202 – Jewellers, jewellery and watch repairers | GTR Immigration Creative precision and technical mastery define the professionals under NOC 62202. These experts are responsible for the fabrication, assembly, appraisal, and intricate repair of fine jewellery, clocks, and watches. As a TEER 2 occupation, this role is highly valued within Canada’s luxury retail and specialized manufacturing sectors. For immigration, this NOC offers a viable pathway to Permanent Residency (PR) through the Federal Skilled Worker (Express Entry) program and various Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs), particularly for artisans who can combine traditional craftsmanship with modern technology.
duties: Fabricate and repair precious metal jewellery such as rings, brooches, and bracelets.
Examine, cut, shape, and polish diamonds and precious gems using optical instruments and lathes.
Appraise gemstones to identify rare specimens and detect peculiarities affecting value.
Set precious stones in jewellery mountings according to precise specifications.
May supervise or train other jewellers and apprentices.
Disassemble, examine, and repair worn or broken parts in clocks and watches.
Fabricate or fit replacement parts for vintage or high-end timepieces.
Test, adjust, and regulate timepiece movements for accuracy.
Clean delicate parts using ultrasonic or mechanical cleaning machines.
employment_requirements: Completion of secondary school is usually required.
Completion of a college or other program (community college or technical institute) in gemology or jewellery/watch repair is usually required.
Alternatively, completion of an apprenticeship training program of two or more years is often accepted.
Several years of experience as a jewellery or watch assembler may be required to move into specialized repair or appraisal roles.
job_outlook: The outlook for this occupation is Balanced. According to the Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS), the number of job openings (estimated at 1,000) is expected to be closely aligned with the number of new job seekers (700). Retirements will account for a high percentage of openings, providing a steady entry point for international professionals and school leavers.
labour_market_demand: The demand for jewellers and watch repairers is driven by a steady luxury goods market and a significant wave of retirements within the trade. Major urban hubs like Toronto (Ontario) and Vancouver (British Columbia) remain the primary centers for employment, housing over 80% of the industry's retail and manufacturing infrastructure. Employers are increasingly seeking "bench jewellers" who can handle complex repairs and custom designs to meet the rising consumer demand for personalized luxury items.
High Self-Employment Potential: A significant portion of professionals in this NOC successfully operate their own independent studios or repair shops.
Artistic Innovation: Access to advanced technology like CAD (Computer-Aided Design) and laser welding in established Canadian manufacturing firms.
Stable Retail Sector: Work with prestigious global brands and high-end local boutiques across the country.
PR Advantage: This TEER 2 occupation provides a competitive edge in the Express Entry pool, especially when combined with a valid job offer.
pathways: Express Entry (Federal Skilled Worker): As a TEER 2 role, you can qualify with the required language and education credentials.
Ontario Immigrant Nominee Program (OINP): Targetted for those with job offers in the GTA’s vibrant jewellery district.
Saskatchewan INP (Occupations In-Demand): Often a viable route for skilled tradespeople with specialized repair expertise.
CEC (Canadian Experience Class): The fastest pathway for those who have already completed one year of work in a Canadian jewellery store or repair shop.
